title: Database Internals
summary: B-tree indexes, WAL, MVCC, VACUUM, and PostgreSQL internals for writing better queries and maintaining healthy databases.
guide:
# Database Internals

## Why Learn Database Internals?

Developers who understand how their database actually works can:
- Choose the right index type
- Explain why a query suddenly got slow
- Understand what VACUUM does and why you need it
- Know when an ORM is hiding something dangerous

```
Surface level developer: 'Add an index, it will be faster'
After this guide:        'Add a B-tree index on (status, created_at)
                          using CONCURRENTLY so production isn't locked.
                          Expect 20MB overhead. Run ANALYZE after.'
```

---

## B-Tree — The Index Data Structure

```
A B-tree is a self-balancing tree that keeps data sorted,
allowing searches, insertions, and deletions in O(log n).

B-tree node (PostgreSQL default: 8KB pages):

  Page 1 (root):        [30 | 70]
                       /    |    \
  Page 2:           [5|15]  |  [50|60]  ← branch pages
                            |
  Page 3:         ← [30|35|40] →       ← leaf pages (actual data or pointers)

Leaf pages contain: {key, heap_pointer}
  key = the indexed column value
  heap_pointer = {page_number, slot_number} pointing to actual row in table

B-tree search (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 42):
  1. Read root page: 42 < 70? → go left
  2. Read branch: 42 > 30? → go right subtree
  3. Read leaf: find 42 → get heap pointer
  4. Read heap page at {page:5, slot:7} → actual row
  Total: 3-4 page reads vs potentially millions for Seq Scan

B-tree height: log_100(rows) (branching factor ~100 per page)
  1M rows: height = 3
  1B rows: height = 5
  → Very flat, very fast
```

---

## Write-Ahead Log (WAL)

```
QUESTION: If PostgreSQL crashes mid-write, how does it not corrupt data?

ANSWER: Write-Ahead Log

WAL Rule: Before changing any data page, write the INTENT to the WAL log first.
          WAL is sequential I/O (append-only) → very fast even on HDD.

Flow for UPDATE orders SET status='shipped' WHERE id=42:
  1. Write WAL record: 'I am about to change page 47, slot 3, field status'
  2. fsync WAL to disk (durable!)
  3. Modify the in-memory page (buffer pool)
  4. Page eventually flushed to disk (async, batched)

CRASH RECOVERY:
  PostgreSQL restarts → finds uncommitted WAL records → replays them
  Or: finds committed WAL but flushed page is corrupted → replays from WAL
  WAL guarantees durability: 'D' in ACID

WAL also enables:
  Streaming replication: send WAL records to standby replica in real-time
  Point-in-time recovery: replay WAL from backup to any point in time
  Logical replication: decode WAL changes to SQL events (CDC)
```

---

## MVCC — Multi-Version Concurrency Control

```
PROBLEM: Transaction A is reading rows while Transaction B is updating them.
  - Lock B until A finishes? → terrible for read performance
  - Let A see partially updated data? → consistency violated

MVCC SOLUTION: Keep multiple versions of each row.
  Every row version has: xmin (transaction that created it)
                         xmax (transaction that deleted/updated it)

  Original row:  {xmin: 100, xmax: null, status: 'pending'}
  After UPDATE by txn 200:
    Old version: {xmin: 100, xmax: 200, status: 'pending'}  ← now deleted
    New version: {xmin: 200, xmax: null, status: 'shipped'} ← current

Transaction A (started at txid 150):
  Reads row: which version is visible?
  Rule: visible if xmin <= 150 AND (xmax is null OR xmax > 150)
  Old version: xmin=100 ≤ 150, xmax=200 > 150 → VISIBLE to A
  A sees 'pending' even though B already committed 'shipped'

Benefits:
  - Readers never block writers
  - Writers never block readers
  - Each transaction sees a consistent snapshot

Cost: dead tuples accumulate (old versions no longer needed by any transaction)
      Enter: VACUUM
```

---

## VACUUM — Dead Tuple Cleanup

```
Dead tuples = row versions no longer visible to any transaction.
They take space, slow index scans, cause 'table bloat'.

VACUUM reclaims dead tuple space:
  Marks space as reusable (not returned to OS unless VACUUM FULL)
  Updates visibility map (used for index-only scans)
  Prevents transaction ID wraparound (fatal if not done!)

AUTOVACUUM: PostgreSQL runs VACUUM automatically when tables change.
  Tunable: autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 50
            autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.2
  Triggers when: 50 + 20% of table rows are dead

VACUUM FULL: compacts table to minimum size, rewrites entire table.
  Use case: after deleting 90% of rows.
  Cost: takes exclusive lock on table! Use pg_repack instead for zero-downtime.

ANALYZE: updates table statistics (column histograms for query planner).
  Run after large data loads: ANALYZE tablename;

Monitor bloat:
  SELECT relname, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup FROM pg_stat_user_tables
  ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC;
```

---

## Index Types in PostgreSQL

```
+------------------+---------------------------+-------------------------------+
| Index type       | Use case                  | Key feature                   |
+------------------+---------------------------+-------------------------------+
| B-tree (default) | =, <, >, BETWEEN, LIKE    | Equality + range queries      |
| Hash             | = only                    | Faster equality, no range     |
| GIN              | Array containment, JSONB  | Multiple values in one column |
| GiST             | Geometric, full-text, IP  | Generalized search tree       |
| BRIN             | Append-only (time-series) | Very small, block-range only  |
+------------------+---------------------------+-------------------------------+

GIN example (JSONB):
  CREATE INDEX idx_meta ON events USING GIN(metadata);
  SELECT * FROM events WHERE metadata @> '{"type": "click"}';

BRIN example (time-series):
  CREATE INDEX idx_ts ON metrics USING BRIN(created_at);
  -- Stores only min/max per block range
  -- 99% smaller than B-tree, perfect for append-only tables

Partial index (smaller, faster for specific queries):
  CREATE INDEX idx_active ON users(email) WHERE status = 'active';
  -- Only indexes active users, ignores deleted. Smaller = faster.

Concurrent index build (zero locks):
  CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_user ON orders(user_id);
  -- Takes longer but doesn't block read/write. Use in production.
```

---

## Mind Map

```
DATABASE INTERNALS
|
+-- B-TREE
|   +-- Balanced, sorted, O(log n)
|   +-- Leaf pages → heap pointers
|   +-- Height = 3-5 for any practical size
|
+-- WAL
|   +-- Write intent BEFORE changing data
|   +-- Enables: crash recovery, replication, PITR
|   +-- Sequential append-only I/O
|
+-- MVCC
|   +-- Multiple row versions (xmin, xmax)
|   +-- Readers never block writers
|   +-- Creates dead tuples → needs VACUUM
|
+-- VACUUM
|   +-- Reclaims dead tuples
|   +-- AUTOVACUUM runs automatically
|   +-- VACUUM FULL locks table (avoid!)
|
+-- INDEX TYPES
    +-- B-tree: default, =/</>
    +-- GIN: JSONB, arrays
    +-- BRIN: time-series
    +-- Partial: WHERE condition
    +-- CONCURRENTLY: no lock
```

---

## References

### Videos
- **PostgreSQL Internals** by Hussein Nasser: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q8IEtTfwfVQ
- **How Databases Work Internally** by CMU Database Group: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLSE8ODhjZXjbj8BMuIrRcacnQh20hmY9g

### Books
- **Database Internals** by Alex Petrov — excellent deep dive
- **PostgreSQL 14 Internals** (free PDF): https://postgrespro.com/community/books/internals

questions.prompt: A B-tree index on a 1-billion row table — approximately how many disk page reads to find one row?
choices: log_100(1B) ≈ 5 page reads — B-tree branching factor ~100 per page, height stays very flat even at massive scale
questions.explanation: B-tree height = log_branch_factor(N). PostgreSQL page = 8KB, can hold ~100+ key-pointer pairs. log100(1B) = log(1B)/log(100) = 9/2 = 4.5 → ~5 levels. Even for 1B rows, B-tree height rarely exceeds 5 reads. That's why indexes are transformatively fast.

questions.prompt: Write-Ahead Log (WAL) — what must happen before data pages can be modified?
choices: WAL record describing the change must be written and durably fsynced to disk first. Only then can the in-memory buffer pool be modified. If crash occurs before page flush, WAL enables recovery.
questions.explanation: WAL guarantees: 'D' in ACID (durability). Write intent to WAL (sequential, fast) → modify memory → flush memory to disk eventually (async). If crash between WAL and disk flush: WAL has the intent, replay it on restart. Sequential WAL writes are much faster than random heap writes.

questions.prompt: What are 'dead tuples' in PostgreSQL?
choices: Row versions that are no longer visible to any active transaction — created by UPDATE (old version) and DELETE. They consume space and slow queries until VACUUM reclaims them.
questions.explanation: UPDATE in PostgreSQL doesn't modify in-place. It marks old version deleted (xmax set) and inserts new version. Old version is a 'dead tuple' once no transaction can see it. Without VACUUM, dead tuples accumulate causing table bloat and slower sequential scans.

questions.prompt: MVCC allows readers to never block writers because:
choices: Readers read old row versions (based on their transaction snapshot) while writers create new versions. Both access different versions simultaneously — no lock contention between readers and writers.
questions.explanation: MVCC: each row version has xmin/xmax transaction IDs. Readers consult visibility rules (is xmin ≤ my txid AND xmax not set or > my txid?) to decide which version to read. Writer creates new version. They access different memory locations — no lock needed.

questions.prompt: When should you use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY over regular CREATE INDEX?
choices: In production systems where you can't afford table locks. Regular CREATE INDEX takes ShareLock (blocks writes). CONCURRENTLY doesn't lock but takes 2-3x longer and requires more work. Use CONCURRENTLY for live production tables.
CONCURRENTLY doesn't work on large tables
questions.explanation: Regular CREATE INDEX: ShareLock on table — allows reads, blocks writes. For large tables this could take minutes/hours with writes blocked. CONCURRENTLY: does two passes, allows normal read/write throughout, takes longer but zero downtime. Standard practice for production index creation.

questions.prompt: What does ANALYZE do in PostgreSQL?
choices: Collects statistics about column value distributions (histograms, most-common values, null fraction) — query planner uses these to estimate rows and choose execution plan. Run after bulk data changes.
questions.explanation: ANALYZE samples table rows, builds histograms per column. Planner uses these to estimate: 'WHERE status=shipped' → maybe 30% of rows. With stale stats: planner estimates 10 rows, does Nested Loop. Actual: 10M rows, should use Hash Join. ANALYZE = accurate estimates = good plans.

questions.prompt: When is a GIN index better than a B-tree index?
choices: GIN excels at multi-value columns: JSONB fields, arrays, full-text search. A row can have multiple indexed values (e.g., tags array). B-tree handles only single scalar comparisons. GIN: one row maps to many index entries.
questions.explanation: B-tree: one index entry per row. Perfect for single-value columns. GIN: multiple entries per row. Used for: JSONB containment (@>), array containment (@>), full-text (@@). Slower to build, larger, but enabling queries that B-tree simply can't do efficiently.

questions.prompt: What is a heap in PostgreSQL database internals?
choices: The main table storage — unordered collection of 8KB pages containing row data. Indexes point into the heap via (page_number, slot_number). Unlike clustered tables in MySQL, PostgreSQL heap is unordered.
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL heap = physical table storage. 8KB pages, each holding multiple row versions (tuples). Index leaf entries contain a 6-byte heap tuple ID (ctid): {page_number, slot_within_page}. Index scan: follow index → ctid → fetch from heap. Index-only scan: never touches heap if all columns are in index.

questions.prompt: Transaction ID wraparound in PostgreSQL — why is it dangerous?
choices: PostgreSQL transaction IDs are 32-bit integers (max ~2 billion). If wraparound occurs without VACUUM cleaning old txids, rows committed before the wrap look 'in the future' — database refuses to start to prevent data corruption.
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL uses txid for MVCC visibility. 32-bit txid: wraps after ~2.1 billion transactions. If VACUUM doesn't freeze old rows before wraparound, the DB shuts down to prevent showing wrong data to users. Age shown in: SELECT age(relfrozenxid) FROM pg_class. Alert when age approaches 200M.

questions.prompt: What enables streaming replication in PostgreSQL?
choices: WAL records are sent from primary to standby replicas in real-time. Standby replays WAL records to stay current. Primary writes WAL anyway for durability — streaming is essentially a zero-cost side effect of the WAL.
questions.explanation: Streaming replication: WAL records generated on primary are sent via TCP to standbys. Standbys replay WAL in recovery mode. This is synchronous (primary waits for ack before committing, wal_sync_method=remote_apply) or async. Read replicas serve read queries while primary handles writes.

questions.prompt: VACUUM vs VACUUM FULL — when should you use VACUUM FULL?
choices: VACUUM FULL: rewrites entire table to reclaim disk space back to OS. Takes exclusive lock (table unusable during). Use only when table has huge bloat (80%+ dead) and offline maintenance window is acceptable. Use pg_repack for zero-downtime compaction.
questions.explanation: Regular VACUUM: marks dead space reusable but doesn't return to OS. Disk usage stays same. VACUUM FULL: physically rewrites table (like CLUSTER), disk space returned. But: takes AccessExclusiveLock — all reads and writes blocked. In production: schedule maintenance window or use pg_repack which does it without locks.

questions.prompt: What is a 'covering index' also called in PostgreSQL 11+?
choices: INCLUDE index — CREATE INDEX idx ON orders(user_id) INCLUDE (amount, status). user_id is the search key; amount and status are stored in leaf pages but not part of the tree. Enables index-only scans for queries fetching these columns.
questions.explanation: INCLUDE clause adds non-key columns to leaf pages without making them part of the B-tree search structure. SELECT amount, status FROM orders WHERE user_id=42: index scan finds user_id=42, gets amount+status from same page — no heap fetch. Eliminates the 'heap lookup' step.

questions.prompt: PostgreSQL checkpoint — what is it?
choices: Point where all dirty buffer pages (modified in memory but not yet on disk) are flushed to disk. Checkpoint records this stable point in WAL. Recovery after crash: replay WAL from last checkpoint only (not entire WAL history).
questions.explanation: Without checkpoints: crash recovery replays WAL from beginning (could be hours). Checkpoints write all dirty pages to disk at regular intervals. Post-crash: replay only WAL after last checkpoint. Trade-off: more frequent checkpoints = faster recovery, more I/O. Configured via checkpoint_completion_target.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between MVCC in PostgreSQL and MySQL InnoDB?
choices: PostgreSQL: stores old row versions in the same heap (all versions in main table). InnoDB: stores old versions in a separate 'undo log' segment, main clustered index always has latest version. Both achieve MVCC but different storage trade-offs.
questions.explanation: PostgreSQL heap-based MVCC: dead versions pile up in-place, need VACUUM. InnoDB undo-log MVCC: clustered index is always latest, old versions in separate undo tablespace (auto-compacted). InnoDB: no VACUUM equivalent needed. PostgreSQL: needs autovacuum. Different architectural trade-offs.

questions.prompt: BRIN index vs B-tree — when is BRIN appropriate?
choices: BRIN (Block Range Index) is tiny and fast for columns that are physically ordered in storage — e.g., auto-increment IDs, timestamps in append-only tables. Stores just min/max per block range. 100-1000x smaller than B-tree. Awful for random data.
questions.explanation: BRIN assumes correlation between column order and physical storage order. For time-series (INSERT always appends, timestamp always increasing): BRIN is perfect. Just store min/max timestamp per 128 pages. WHERE created_at > yesterday: BRIN quickly excludes old blocks. Random UUIDs: BRIN useless (no correlation).

questions.prompt: What does pg_stat_user_tables.n_dead_tup tell you?
choices: Number of dead (obsolete MVCC) tuple versions in the table not yet claimed by VACUUM. High values indicate autovacuum is behind or disabled. Should trigger manual VACUUM if approaching significant % of live tuples.
questions.explanation: n_dead_tup: current count of dead tuples awaiting VACUUM. Formula: if n_dead_tup > autovacuum_vacuum_threshold + autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor * n_live_tup, autovacuum will trigger. Monitor this to catch tables autovacuum can't keep up with (high-write tables).

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of PostgreSQL's shared_buffers setting?
choices: Size of PostgreSQL's buffer pool — pages read from disk are cached here. Reads served from buffer pool: no disk I/O. Hit rate should be >99%. Rule of thumb: 25% of available RAM. Too small: thrashing. Too large: leaves no RAM for OS page cache.
questions.explanation: shared_buffers is PostgreSQL's main cache. 8KB pages cached in shared memory. SELECT reads page from disk into shared_buffers, serves from there on subsequent reads. Check hit rate: SELECT sum(heap_blks_hit)/(sum(heap_blks_hit)+sum(heap_blks_read)) FROM pg_statio_user_tables. Target: 0.99+.

questions.prompt: Logical replication vs WAL streaming replication — key difference?
choices: WAL streaming: sends raw binary WAL records, must be same PostgreSQL major version, replicates everything. Logical: decodes WAL into SQL-level changes (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), can replicate subset of tables, works across versions and to other databases.
questions.explanation: Streaming: binary WAL → standby must match primary's exact version and architecture. All or nothing. Logical: decoded as table row changes → can subscribe from different PG version, or stream to Kafka, Debezium CDC. Used for: blue-green upgrades, event streaming, selective table replication.

questions.prompt: What problem does the 'table bloat' cause for query performance?
choices: Sequential scan must read ALL pages including those with only dead tuples — larger physical table even if logically small. Index size also bloats. 100% live rows in 2x table size = 2x scan time.
questions.explanation: Bloat: table's physical size > logical data size due to dead tuples. Seq scan: reads every page regardless of dead/live. 1M table with 50% dead = physical table of 2M dead rows. Reads 2x the data. VACUUM reclaims space (marks reusable). VACUUM FULL compacts physically.

questions.prompt: Index-only scan in PostgreSQL — when can it avoid touching the heap?
choices: When all columns in the SELECT and WHERE are in the index (covering index) AND the visibility map marks the page as all-visible. PostgreSQL then skips the heap entirely — returns data from index pages only.
questions.explanation: Index-only scan: conditions: 1) All needed columns in index (INCLUDE or part of key). 2) Page's visibility map bit set to 'all-visible' (VACUUM sets this when page has no dead tuples). If VM bit not set: must visit heap to check row visibility. VACUUM keeps VM up-to-date, enabling index-only scans.

flashcards.front: B-tree height for any row count
flashcards.back: Height = ceil(log_100(N)). 1M rows: 3 levels. 1B rows: 5 levels. Each level = 1 page read. B-tree is always at most 5-6 disk reads for any realistic table. That's why indexes transform performance from O(n) to O(log n).

Write intent to WAL (sequential, fast) BEFORE modifying data pages. Crash recovery: replay WAL from last checkpoint. Side effects: streaming replication, point-in-time recovery, logical decoding. WAL is the source of truth for PostgreSQL durability.

Row visible if: xmin ≤ current_txid AND (xmax is null OR xmax > current_txid). xmin = transaction that created row. xmax = transaction that deleted/updated it. Enables readers to see consistent snapshots without locking.

autovacuum fires when: n_dead_tup > vacuum_threshold + scale_factor * n_live_tup. Default: 50 + 20% of table. For high-write tables: lower scale_factor. Monitor: SELECT relname, n_dead_tup, last_autovacuum FROM pg_stat_user_tables.

B-tree: default, equality + range. Hash: equality only (rarely better than B-tree). GIN: JSONB, arrays, full-text. GiST: geometric, IP ranges. BRIN: time-series append-only tables (tiny, O(blocks) reads). Partial: WHERE condition reduces size.

ALWAYS: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY in production. Regular CREATE INDEX = ShareLock (blocks writes for minutes/hours on large tables). CONCURRENTLY takes 2-3x longer but zero impact on live traffic. Cannot be inside explicit transactions.

SELECT schemaname, relname, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup, last_autovacuum FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_dead_tup > 10000 ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC. Bloat ratio: n_dead_tup/n_live_tup > 0.2 = investigate autovacuum settings.

Checkpoint = flush all dirty pages to disk. Marks position in WAL. Crash recovery: only replay WAL AFTER last checkpoint. More frequent = faster recovery, more I/O. checkpoint_completion_target=0.9 spreads I/O over 90% of checkpoint_timeout interval.

project.brief: Your PostgreSQL database serving 10 million users has developed serious performance problems: queries that took 10ms now take 5 seconds. Table orders has 500M rows. Diagnose and fix: identify the slow query, check for missing indexes, identify table bloat, verify statistics freshness, and create a maintenance strategy.

checklist.text: EXPLAIN ANALYZE on slow query. Identify: Seq Scan on large table, wrong row estimates, missing index recommendation.
Create appropriate index type: B-tree for WHERE user_id + ORDER BY created_at. CONCURRENTLY to avoid production lock.
Check pg_stat_user_tables for n_dead_tup. If n_dead_tup/n_live_tup > 0.1: tune autovacuum or run manual VACUUM ANALYZE.
Verify statistics freshness: last_analyze timestamp in pg_stat_user_tables. Run ANALYZE if stale (row estimates wrong by 10x).
Maintenance schedule: autovacuum tune for high-write tables (lower scale_factor=0.02), checkpoint alerts, txid wraparound monitoring.

hints: pg_stat_statements extension tracks cumulative query statistics: total_time, calls, mean_time. Install to identify worst queries.
Partial index win: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_pending ON orders(user_id, created_at) WHERE status='pending' — orders without status='pending' excluded.
CLUSTER orders USING idx_orders_user_created (rewrites table in index order) — only if you can afford the downtime. Dramatically improves range scans.
